1: mimaʿămaqîm qĕrāʾtîkā yhwh — "from the depths I cry to you, O LORD" — maʿămaqîm (depths, plural of māʿāmaq, from ʿāmaq, to be deep) — the absolute bottom, the lowest imaginable point; used of deep waters (Psalms 69:2,14; Psalms 88:7), and here of existential distress. The cry is upward from the bottom: YHWH is above; the psalmist is below. The Latin translation de profundis gave this psalm its traditional liturgical name. The singular voice of v1 broadens in vv7-8 to include all Israel — individual experience becomes communal.
2: ʾădōnāy šimʿāh bĕqôlî tihyênāh ʾoznêkā qaššûbôt lĕqôl taḥănûnāy — "Lord, hear my voice; let your ears be attentive to my pleading voice" — the address shifts from yhwh (the covenant name, v1) to ʾădōnāy (the sovereignty name) — the psalmist addresses YHWH's lordship now rather than covenant relationship. qaššûbôt (attentive, listening, Qal participle of qāšab, to pay attention, prick up the ears) — the ears tuned and oriented toward the prayer. taḥănûnāy (my supplications, pleas for grace/mercy, from ḥānan) — same word as Psalms 116:1; Psalms 28:2.
3: ʾim-ʿăwōnôt tišmār-yāh ʾădōnāy mî yaʿămōd — "if you, O LORD, kept a record of sins, Lord, who could stand?" — šāmar ʿăwōnôt (to keep/guard/watch iniquities) — to maintain a legal ledger of sins. mî yaʿămōd (who would stand?) — to "stand" before God is to survive the court's verdict, to remain upright in judgment. The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. The statement is profoundly theological: if divine mercy were replaced by strict accounting, even the most righteous person would be unable to stand. This anticipates Paul's argument in Romans 3.
4: kî-ʿimĕkā hassĕlîḥāh lĕmaʿan tiwwārēʾ — "but forgiveness is found with you — and that is why you are held in awe" — sĕlîḥāh (forgiveness, pardon) — appears only three times in the OT: here, Nehemiah 9:17, and Daniel 9:9 — all in contexts of profound penitence before YHWH. The rarity marks it as a significant, non-routine word. The startling theological argument: YHWH is feared (yārēʾ) precisely because he forgives, not because he judges. If he maintained strict accounts (v3), he would terrify. But his forgiveness produces a deeper awe — the awe of the one who knows they have been spared.
5: qiwwîtî yhwh qiwĕtāh napšî ûlidĕbārô hôḥāltî — "I wait for the LORD — my whole being waits — and in his word I put my hope" — qāwāh (to wait, hope, expect, look for — from a root meaning to stretch like a cord toward something) — intense, expectant waiting. The doubled verb (qiwwîtî + qiwĕtāh napšî) with the soul (nepeš) as subject: not only the mind but the whole living person is stretched toward YHWH. lidĕbārô hôḥāltî (in his word I hope, Hiphil of yāḥal) — YHWH's spoken word is the ground of the hope; this is not wishful thinking but expectation anchored in promise.
6: napšî layhwāh miššōmĕrîm labboqer šōmĕrîm labboqer — "my soul waits for the LORD more than those who watch for the morning, more than those who watch for the morning" — šōmĕrîm labboqer (those who watch for the morning, dawn-sentinels) — night watchmen on the city walls who wait for the first light of dawn to signal the end of their vigilance. The comparison and its repetition: the psalmist's longing for YHWH is more intense than the watchman's longing for dawn. The repeated phrase ("more than those who watch for the morning... those who watch for the morning") is unusual — the MT double-occurrence is generally considered deliberate emphasis, not scribal error.
7: yaḥēl yiśrāʾēl ʾel-yhwh kî-ʿim-yhwh haḥesed wĕharbēh ʿimmô pĕdût — "O Israel, put your hope in the LORD, for with the LORD is steadfast love, and with him is abundant redemption" — the psalm broadens from individual (vv1-6) to communal address. haḥesed (the steadfast love, with the definite article — this specific, well-known ḥesed) + harbēh pĕdût (abundant/plentiful redemption) — pĕdût (redemption by payment, from pādāh) with the adjective harbēh (much, abundant) — not merely one act of redemption but overflowing redemption. The contrast with v3: YHWH could keep accounts (mī yaʿămod = no one), but instead he offers abundant redemption.
8: wĕhûʾ yipdeh ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mikkōl ʿăwōnōtāyw — "he will redeem Israel from all its sins" — the comprehensive scope: kōl ʿăwōnōtāyw (all its iniquities) — the same word as v3's ʿăwōnôt (iniquities kept on record). What YHWH chose not to keep as a legal record (v3) he will instead redeem (pādāh, ransom, buy out). The movement of the psalm: from the depths of sin (v1) → impossible standing under strict accounting (v3) → forgiveness (v4) → waiting (vv5-6) → communal hope (v7) → comprehensive redemption (v8). The psalm is a miniature Gospel: sin, judgment, mercy, hope, redemption.
